Understanding TypeScript Generics

TypeScript generics are a powerful feature that allow you to write flexible, reusable functions and classes. Instead of committing to a single type, generics let you write code that works with a variety of types while still maintaining full type safety.

Consider a simple identity function. Without generics, you might write separate functions for strings and numbers. With generics, you write one function that handles any type. The type parameter acts as a placeholder that gets filled in at the call site.

Generics shine in collection types. Arrays, maps, and sets are all generic. When you declare an array of strings in TypeScript, you are using a generic type. The Array type accepts a type parameter that constrains what values can be stored inside it.

Constraints further refine generics. Using the extends keyword you can require that a type parameter must have certain properties. This gives you the flexibility of generics while still allowing you to call methods on the type parameter inside the function body.

Understanding generics unlocks the full power of TypeScript. Once you are comfortable with them you will find yourself reaching for them whenever you need to write reusable, type-safe utilities that work across multiple data shapes.

Published 2024. All rights reserved.
